# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bullet + sub-bullets list of the development
"ladder". Each step starts with a `[[N]]` reference slot —
replaced with the step's commit ref on first backfill, once
the step is pushed — then the version, the title, and a
`(done|current)` marker as appropriate:
   - [[N]] 0.xx.y-0 blah (current)
   - [[N]] 0.xx.y-1 blah blah
   - [[N]] 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - [[N]] 0.xx.y close-out and validation

At close-out the ladder lives on as the cycle's chores
`### As-built ladder`, where the refs are preserved
(renumbered to that file's local `# References` slots).

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run TODO.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. **Buffer-swap servicing model.** Today a probe has one
   histogram recorded to completion. Future: a 1-bit
   "needs service soon" signal per probe; a background task
   hands over a fresh zeroed buffer and takes the full one
   for analysis. Deferred with no hard decisions; the
   config/storage split keeps it open. See [[1]].
2. **tprobe adoption.** Replace `hdrhistogram` in tprobe's
   `examples/tp_pc`, then adopt in its core recording path.
   The integration cycle runs in tprobe's repo; this entry
   tracks API gaps it surfaces here.

## Ideas

- Interval snapshot / reset API (monitoring cadence; makes
  u32-vs-u64 count-width pressure moot).
- Atomic concurrent recording via `portable-atomic`.
- HdrHistogram V2 wire/log format compatibility (zigzag
  LEB128 is `no_std`-fine; compression needs a dep).
- `alloc` feature: Vec-backed storage, auto-resize.

content.text:
The comparison is done. I added an iopsystems `histogram` crate variant to `benches/record.rs` (it was already a dev-dependency as the counts-parity oracle, and its `(g, n)` constructor matches our config directly) and ran the bench twice. Results were stable across runs:

| variant | ns/record |
|---|---|
| raw streaming store | 0.87 |
| **h2hist u32** | **2.57** |
| h2hist u32 + min/max/sum extras | 3.30 |
| **histogram crate (iopsystems)** | **1.82** |
| **hdrhistogram 2sf** | **4.94** |

(g=7, n=30, 3072 buckets, 8M heavy-tailed samples, best of 3 passes.)

Takeaways:

- h2hist records about 2× faster than hdrhistogram at comparable precision (g=7 ≈ 0.78% relative error vs 2 significant figures ≈ 1%).
- The iopsystems crate — which uses the same h2 index math — is about 0.75 ns/record faster than us. Our guess is the gap is the work we do per record that it doesn't: it does a plain `wrapping_add` on a u64 counter and tracks nothing else, while our `record_into` (src/histogram.rs:20) does a saturating add on the counter *and* maintains the running `total` with a second saturating add, adding a dependency chain per record. The index computation itself is essentially identical between the two.
- The inline min/max/sum extras cost ~0.73 ns on top of recording — that's the standing data point for the parked hot-path-extras decision.

The bench edit is sitting uncommitted in the work repo (`benches/record.rs`: new variant plus a doc-comment line); fmt and clippy are clean. If you want to keep it, it can go in a small cycle — and if the 0.75 ns gap versus iopsystems matters for tprobe, the obvious experiment is a variant that drops the `total` bookkeeping from the hot path and recomputes it at read time.

The plain version: recording one value into h2hist takes about two and a half billionths of a second — twice as fast as the standard HdrHistogram library, but a bit slower than the iopsystems crate, most likely because we also keep a running "how many samples total" tally on every record while they don't.

Based on that logic we do the total after the fact. My 2 cents.

# Architecture

This file uses [Prose form](AGENTS.md#prose-form). It records
the design choices for the crate and the 0.1.0 starting point.
Code structure sections will grow as the ladder lands; the
choices below are the founding state (2026-07-21, pre-scaffold).

## Overview

The crate is a `no_std`, no-alloc reimplementation of the
HdrHistogram log-linear bucketing scheme (Gil Tene et al.,
[HdrHistogram](https://github.com/HdrHistogram/HdrHistogram)),
sized and shaped for tprobe-class continuous monitoring: tens
to hundreds of probes per process, each recording tick-counter
deltas run-to-completion, analyzed at the end of the run.

- The one hot operation is `record(value)`: find the bucket
  (clz + shift), saturating-increment its counter. No floats,
  no allocation, no panics, no data-dependent work.
- Everything else — quantiles, merge, iteration — is
  O(buckets), `no_std`-capable, and off the measured path.
- Lifecycle for 0.1.0 is run-to-completion: create, record,
  analyze at end. No snapshot/swap/reset API yet (see
  [Deferred directions](#deferred-directions)).

## Parameterization (h2)

The h2 restatement of HdrHistogram's bucketing — borrowed from
iopsystems' [histogram](https://github.com/iopsystems/histogram)
crate, written up at [h2histogram.org](https://h2histogram.org/)
— replaces `(lowest, highest, significant_figures)` with
two powers, chosen per instance at runtime:

- `grouping_power` (g) — each power-of-two range is split
  into 2ᵍ linear sub-buckets; relative value error ≤ 2⁻ᵍ.
- `max_value_power` (n) — max trackable value is 2ⁿ−1;
  over-range values clamp into the top bucket (for lateness
  detection the overflow *is* the signal).
- Values below 2ᵍ⁺¹ are recorded exactly.
- `buckets = (n − g + 1) · 2ᵍ`.

## Storage: config / counts split

The load-bearing structural choice: config and counts storage
are separate, and the core histogram type *borrows* its counts.

- `Config` — the two powers plus derived index math; `const fn`
  so callers can size storage at compile time.
- `Histogram<'a, C>` — config + `&'a mut [C]` counts + totals.
  The caller owns the memory: a static buffer, a stack array,
  or (with `std`) a heap slice.
- `HistogramArray<const N, C>` — owned inline `[C; N]`
  convenience wrapper with a size check against
  `Config::total_buckets()`. Rust's stable const generics
  can't derive N from (g, n) directly (`generic_const_exprs`
  is unstable), so N is explicit and checked.
- Why borrowing is the default: the future buffer-swap model —
  a background task handing a probe a fresh zeroed slice and
  taking the full one — needs detachable storage. Keeping
  config and counts unfused now means that model lands later
  without reshaping the type.

## Counts

- Counter type is generic (`u8`/`u16`/`u32`/`u64`) with
  **u32 the default**; increments saturate — no panic, no
  wraparound lies.
- We think u32 is the right monitoring default because a
  deployment that watches stability will bound per-histogram
  totals (by run length now, by snapshot interval later);
  u64 is for run-forever accumulation and doubles the
  footprint for it.

## Feature map

- Core: `#![cfg_attr(not(feature = "std"), no_std)]`,
  no dependencies.
- `std` (default): convenience only — the demo example and
  any std-needing helpers. Band-table reporting stays in
  tprobe; this crate does not duplicate it.
- Dev-dependencies (tests/benches only): iopsystems
  `histogram` and `hdrhistogram` 7 as correctness oracles,
  plus a bench harness.

## API sketch (provisional)

```rust
pub struct Config { /* grouping_power, max_value_power */ }
impl Config {
    pub const fn new(g: u8, n: u8) -> Result<Config, Error>;
    pub const fn total_buckets(&self) -> usize;
    pub const fn index_for(&self, value: u64) -> usize;
    pub const fn value_range(&self, index: usize) -> (u64, u64);
}

pub struct Histogram<'a, C: Counter = u32> {
    // config, total: u64, counts: &'a mut [C]
}
impl<C: Counter> Histogram<'_, C> {
    pub fn record(&mut self, value: u64);
    pub fn record_n(&mut self, value: u64, count: u64);
    pub fn quantile(&self, q: f64) -> Option<u64>;
    pub fn merge_from(&mut self, other: &Self) -> Result<(), Error>;
    pub fn buckets(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = Bucket>;
}

pub struct HistogramArray<const N: usize, C: Counter = u32> {
    // owned [C; N], checked against Config::total_buckets()
}
```

## Size tradeoffs

`bytes = (n − g + 1) · 2ᵍ · sizeof(C)` + ~48 B header
(total/min/max/config).

What a max value of 2ⁿ ticks spans in time depends on the tick
source's rate:

| n  | 100 Hz  | 1 kHz   | 3 GHz   |
|----|---------|---------|---------|
| 16 | ~11 min | ~66 s   | ~22 µs  |
| 20 | ~2.9 h  | ~17 min | ~350 µs |
| 24 | ~1.9 d  | ~4.7 h  | ~5.6 ms |
| 32 | ~1.4 y  | ~50 d   | ~1.4 s  |
| 36 | ~22 y   | ~2.2 y  | ~23 s   |
| 40 | ~349 y  | ~35 y   | ~6 min  |

A slow tick source therefore wants a much smaller n, which
shrinks the footprint directly: e.g. a 1 kHz tick watching
1 ms..65 s lateness needs only n = 16 — at g = 4/u32 that is
(16−4+1)·2⁴ = 208 buckets ≈ 832 B per probe.

| g  | n  | C   | rel. err | buckets | bytes   |
|----|----|-----|----------|---------|---------|
| 2  | 32 | u16 | 25%      | 124     | ~250 B  |
| 4  | 36 | u32 | 6.3%     | 528     | ~2.1 KB |
| 6  | 40 | u32 | 1.6%     | 2,240   | ~9 KB   |
| 7  | 40 | u32 | 0.8%     | 4,352   | ~17 KB  |
| 7  | 40 | u64 | 0.8%     | 4,352   | ~34 KB  |
| 10 | 40 | u32 | 0.1%     | 31,744  | ~124 KB |

- rel. err is the value-quantization bound 2⁻ᵍ: each
  power-of-two range holds 2ᵍ equal-width buckets, so a
  bucket spans ≤ 2⁻ᵍ of the values in it. Counts are exact;
  only the value axis is quantized — any value read back
  (quantile, boundary) is within 2⁻ᵍ of the recorded one.
  Values below 2ᵍ⁺¹ are exact (width-1 buckets).
  - 2⁻ᵍ is the full uncertainty band: reporting a bucket
    endpoint gives one-sided error up to 2⁻ᵍ; reporting the
    midpoint halves it to ±2⁻ᵍ⁄2. Which convention
    `quantile()` uses is decided at 0.1.0-5 (oracle parity
    with `hdrhistogram`'s upper-bound convention weighs in).
- g=7/u32 is parity with `hdrhistogram` at 2 significant
  figures; g=10 approximates 3.
- We think g=4..6 is the monitoring sweet spot: error is on
  the *value* axis, and flagging a p99.9 that moved 10× does
  not need 2-sigfig fidelity.
- Fleet math: 100 probes at g=6/n=40/u32 ≈ 900 KB per
  process; double it if a snapshot model later
  double-buffers each probe.

## Validation

## Readout requirements (band tables)

The consumer-side bar is iiac-perf's band table: z/p/n quantile
bands, each showing first/last/range/count/mean, plus overall
and quantile-trimmed mean/stdev. Everything derives from the
bucket iterator:

- first/last/range/count — band's first/last non-empty bucket
  bounds and summed counts; bands are quantile fences, so the
  iteration API must make **cumulative** counts easy.
- mean/stdev (overall, per-band, trimmed) — bucket-midpoint
  weighted, accurate to rel. err; original HdrHistogram
  computes them the same way.
- exact overall mean would need a running `sum` at record
  time (one u64 add) — a hot-path candidate decided by bench
  at 0.1.0-8, alongside exact min/max.
- calibration adjustment (iiac-perf's "adjusted" column) is
  presentation-layer; stays in the consumer.

## Validation

- Exhaustive small-config tests: for tiny (g, n), walk every
  value in 0..2ⁿ, assert indices are monotone and bucket
  ranges exactly partition the domain.
- Index-parity oracle: iopsystems `histogram` uses the same
  h2 scheme, so `index_for` must match exactly.
- Quantile-parity oracle: `hdrhistogram` 7 on identical
  random streams, agreeing within equivalent-value tolerance
  (the g↔sigfig mapping is approximate).
- `no_std` proof: a check script building
  `--no-default-features` for a bare-metal target.
- Record-path bench: ns/record vs a raw array store and vs
  `hdrhistogram`. Outcome (0.1.0-8): 2.6 ns/record vs
  hdrhistogram's 4.9; exact min/max/sum would add ~28%, so
  they stay **off** the hot path (bench numbers and decision
  in chores-01).

## Deferred directions

Kept open, not designed (see TODO.md):

- Buffer-swap servicing: per-probe 1-bit "needs service soon"
  signal; a background task swaps a fresh buffer for the full
  one. Enabled by the config/storage split.
- Interval snapshot / reset; atomic concurrent recording
  (`portable-atomic`); HdrHistogram V2 wire format; `alloc`
  feature (Vec-backed storage, auto-resize).

## See also

- [`README.md`](README.md) — user-facing overview and goals.
- [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) — bot workflow, versioning,
  commit/push conventions, code conventions.
- [`TODO.md`](TODO.md) — live task list and the 0.1.0 ladder.
- [`notes/chores/`](notes/chores) — chores-*.md files contain
  discussion and notes on various chores in github compatible
  markdown.

  a background task handing a probe a fresh zeroed slice and
  taking the full one — needs detachable storage. Keeping
  config and counts unfused now means that model lands later
  without reshaping the type.

  wraparound lies.

  deployment that watches stability will bound per-histogram
  totals (by run length now, by snapshot interval later);

|----|---------|---------|---------|

| 20 | ~2.9 h  | ~17 min | ~350 µs |
| 24 | ~1.9 d  | ~4.7 h  | ~5.6 ms |
| 32 | ~1.4 y  | ~50 d   | ~1.4 s  |
| 36 | ~22 y   | ~2.2 y  | ~23 s   |
| 40 | ~349 y  | ~35 y   | ~6 min  |

shrinks the footprint directly: e.g. a 1 kHz tick watching
1 ms..65 s lateness needs only n = 16 — at g = 4/u32 that is
(16−4+1)·2⁴ = 208 buckets ≈ 832 B per probe.

| g  | n  | C   | rel. err | buckets | bytes   |
|----|----|-----|----------|---------|---------|
| 2  | 32 | u16 | 25%      | 124     | ~250 B  |
| 4  | 36 | u32 | 6.3%     | 528     | ~2.1 KB |
| 6  | 40 | u32 | 1.6%     | 2,240   | ~9 KB   |
| 7  | 40 | u32 | 0.8%     | 4,352   | ~17 KB  |
| 7  | 40 | u64 | 0.8%     | 4,352   | ~34 KB  |
| 10 | 40 | u32 | 0.1%     | 31,744  | ~124 KB |

  power-of-two range holds 2ᵍ equal-width buckets, so a
  bucket spans ≤ 2⁻ᵍ of the values in it. Counts are exact;

  Values below 2ᵍ⁺¹ are exact (width-1 buckets).

    endpoint gives one-sided error up to 2⁻ᵍ; reporting the
    midpoint halves it to ±2⁻ᵍ⁄2. Which convention

    with `hdrhistogram`'s upper-bound convention weighs in).

  figures; g=10 approximates 3.

  the *value* axis, and flagging a p99.9 that moved 10× does
  not need 2-sigfig fidelity.

  process; double it if a snapshot model later
  double-buffers each probe.

aiTitle: Compare performance between h2hist, histogram and HdrHistogram

content.text:
The change is in and measured. Results, with the confound resolved:

| variant | ns/record |
|---|---|
| h2hist (total removed) | 2.35 |
| h2hist + total tracked in loop (the old hot path) | 2.58 |
| h2hist + min/max/sum extras | 3.07 |
| histogram crate (io) | 1.62–1.88 across binaries |
| hdrhistogram 2sf | ~5.0 |

Two findings worth calling out:

- **The total cost is real: ~0.23 ns/record**, confirmed cleanly by the same-binary "u32 + total" variant, and matching the before/after delta (2.57 → 2.36). Your call was right — dropping it from the hot path bought about 9%.
- **The iopsystems number is layout-noisy.** It swung 1.62 → 1.88 between binaries with zero changes to its code — recompiling shifts code alignment. So the remaining gap to it is somewhere in 0.5–0.7 ns, not precisely measurable with this harness. We think what's left is our saturating counter add versus its wrapping u64 add, but that's the next experiment, not established.

What changed (all validated — fmt, clippy, full test suite, no_std check pass):

- `record_into` (src/histogram.rs:26) is now just index + saturating bucket add; a shared `total_of` helper sums counts at read time.
- `Histogram` and `HistogramArray` lost their `total` field; `total()` is O(buckets), `quantile()` does two passes (sum, then rank), `merge_from`/`Histogram::new` simplified. `as_histogram()` is now O(1).
- One semantic change: `total()` is now the saturating sum of bucket counts, so once a counter saturates, total no longer counts the overflow records. The saturation test was updated to pin that; it's the cleaner invariant (total always agrees with the counts, which also made the quantile fallback genuinely unreachable).
- ARCHITECTURE.md's struct sketch and size-header note updated; the bench gained the io-crate and "+ total" variants.

Everything is uncommitted in the work repo. This looks like a small cycle — roughly a `feat`/`perf` commit for the total removal plus the bench additions — whenever you want to land it.

The plain version: we stopped updating the running "how many samples" tally on every single record and instead add up the buckets when someone asks. That made each record about 9% faster; the only trade is that asking for the total now takes a quick scan of the 3072 buckets, which is nothing off the hot path.

What's the defintion of a "raw streaming store" and does `h2hist u32` include the totalling or not?

content.text:
**Raw streaming store** (benches/record.rs:82) is the floor baseline: it just writes each sample into the next slot of a pre-allocated 8M-entry `Vec<u64>` — `store[i] = v`, no bucketing, no histogram at all. It's the "ArrayRecorder" pattern of keeping every raw sample, and it represents roughly the cheapest possible per-value memory write on this machine (~0.9 ns). The gap between it and any histogram row is what the bucketing math itself costs.

**`h2hist u32` does not include per-record totalling** — it's the library as it now stands after yesterday's change: index the value, saturating-increment the bucket, nothing else. The `h.total()` call you see in that variant happens once at the end of each 8M-record pass (a single 3072-bucket sum, ~microseconds), purely to keep the optimizer from discarding the work — it's noise at the per-record level.

The **`h2hist u32 + total`** row is the same record path plus a `saturating_add(1)` tally per record in the loop — a stand-in for the old hot path that maintained `total` internally. So the ~0.25 ns between those two rows is exactly what removing the total bought.

The plain version: the first row is just copying numbers into a big array — the speed limit. The `h2hist u32` row is our histogram as-is, with no running tally; the `+ total` row shows what it would cost if we still kept one.

content.text:
Solved — the gap wasn't the counter work at all, it was check ordering in `index_for`. Summary of the investigation:

| variant | before | after |
|---|---|---|
| h2hist u32 | 2.37 | **1.91** |
| h2hist u64 | 2.36 | 1.91 |
| index_for + wrap u32 | 2.35 | 1.90 |
| histogram (u64) | 1.86 | 1.87 |

- The isolation variants ruled out my earlier guess: saturating vs wrapping add and u32 vs u64 counters each cost ~nothing (all three h2hist variants were identical before *and* after).
- The real cost was that `index_for` clamped `value > max_value()` before the exact-region test, so every record — including the ~99% of this stream that takes the exact-region fast path — paid a compare plus a conditional select that the index computation had to wait on. histogram checks the exact region first and only bounds-checks on the log path.
- The fix (src/config.rs:93) reorders: exact-region test first, clamp only on the log path. It's semantics-preserving because over-max values are always ≥ 2^(g+1) when g < n (which `Config::new` enforces), so they can never take the exact-region exit — the full test suite and the counts-parity oracle against histogram both pass, plus fmt/clippy/no_std are clean.

Net for the session: 2.57 → 1.91 ns/record (−26%), and we're now within run-to-run noise of the histogram crate while keeping saturating counters and u32 (half its footprint). The remaining ~0.9 ns over the raw-store floor is the irreducible index math plus the scattered increment.

Uncommitted so far: the `index_for` reorder, the total-removal change, the bench variants/labels, and the small ARCHITECTURE.md updates. Ready to shape into a cycle whenever you want.

The plain version: our histogram was doing its "is this value too big?" safety check on every single sample, even though almost all samples are small and don't need it. Moving that check so it only runs for big values made recording 26% faster — now as fast as the fastest comparable library, while keeping our stricter overflow safety and smaller memory use.

message.content: What happed to hdrhistogram and what is "index_for + wrap u32"?

content.text:
Two separate answers:

**hdrhistogram: nothing happened — it's unchanged, and that's expected.** It's an external crate we didn't touch; its ~4.95 ns has been rock-steady across every run today. It just moved down the table visually because I inserted new rows above it. As to why it's ~2.6× slower than h2hist: its record path does more per sample. Beyond a more involved two-stage index computation (bucket + sub-bucket, with an exponent bias), it maintains extra state on every record — a running total count plus min/max tracking — which is exactly the kind of hot-path bookkeeping we just finished stripping out of h2hist. (The index-math detail I've read in its source before; the exact cost split between the index and the bookkeeping we haven't measured, so treat the "why" as our reading of the code, not a profile.)

**`index_for + wrap u32` is a diagnostic row, not a real API.** It's a hand-rolled loop in the bench (benches/record.rs:119) that calls our public `Config::index_for(v)` to get the bucket, then does a plain wrapping `+= 1` on a bare `Vec<u32>` — i.e., our index math combined with histogram-style "dumb" counter update, skipping the `Histogram` type and its saturating `sat_add` entirely. Its job was to split the h2hist-vs-histogram gap into "index math" vs "counter update": since it benched identical to the full `h2hist u32` row (1.89 vs 1.91), it proved the saturating counter costs nothing measurable and pointed the finger at the index math — which led straight to the check-reordering fix. It's worth keeping in the table as the "index cost floor" reference, but nobody would write that loop in real code.

The plain version: the hdrhistogram line never changed — other rows just got added around it; it's slow because it does extra record-keeping on every sample, like we used to. The `index_for + wrap u32` line isn't a product — it's a test rig we built to figure out *which part* of our code was slow, and it did its job.

**"2sf" = 2 significant figures.** It's the precision argument in the bench's constructor — `hdrhistogram::Histogram::new_with_bounds(1, cfg.max_value(), 2)` (benches/record.rs:137). message.content: It is the `h2hist u32 + total` a optional API or historical?

content.text:
Historical/diagnostic — there's no API behind it. After the change, the library has no total-tracking option on the record path at all; that row is plain bench-loop code that calls `record(v)` and then does its own `total.saturating_add(1)` alongside (benches/record.rs:100), reproducing what the old hot path used to do internally.

It earns its row two ways:

- It documents the measured cost of the decision we just made (~0.23 ns/record), so the "should total live on the hot path?" question has standing data rather than a remembered number.
- It doubles as the price list for a caller who genuinely wants a live running total: the supported pattern is exactly what the row does — keep your own counter next to `record()` — and the row tells them what that costs. (`total()` itself still exists, it just sums the buckets at read time.)

If the table ever feels crowded, that's the first row I'd retire — but I'd keep it until the total-removal cycle lands, since it's the evidence for that commit's rationale.

content.content:
1	# h2hist
2	
3	A `no_std`, no-alloc implementation of iopsystems'
4	[h2 histogram](https://h2histogram.org/) — their base-2
5	redesign of [HdrHistogram](http://hdrhistogram.org/)'s
6	log-linear bucketing — for continuous monitoring of latency,
7	jitter, and real-time lateness. Built to be the core recording
8	structure of [tprobe](../tprobe), but standalone and generally
9	usable; see
10	[Relation to iopsystems' histogram](#relation-to-iopsystems-histogram)
11	for how it differs from their crate.
12	
13	Status: 0.1.0 cycle in progress (see [TODO.md](TODO.md));
14	core, analysis, oracle tests, demo, no_std check, and bench
15	have landed.
16	
17	## Build and test
18	
19	- `cargo build` — host build (std feature on by default).
20	- `cargo build --no-default-features` — the `no_std`
21	  configuration.
22	- `cargo test` — unit tests, doctests, and the oracle parity
23	  suite (dev-deps on `hdrhistogram` and iopsystems
24	  `histogram` as correctness oracles).
25	
26	## Demo
27	
28	`examples/h2demo.rs` records 1M synthetic latency ticks and
29	prints an iiac-perf-style band table (z/p/n quantile bands
30	with first/last/range/count/mean, overall and tail-trimmed
31	mean/stdev):
32	
33	- `cargo run --example h2demo` — run in place.
34	- `cargo install --path . --example h2demo` — install the
35	  demo as a `h2demo` binary.
36	
37	## no_std check and bench
38	
39	- `./scripts/check-no-std.sh` — builds the core
40	  (`--no-default-features`) for every installed bare-metal
41	  (`*-none-*`) target.
42	- `cargo bench` — record-path cost vs a raw store and
43	  `hdrhistogram` (hand-rolled harness; indicative numbers
44	  and the resulting design decision are in
45	  [chores-01](notes/chores/chores-01.md)).
46	
47	## Goals
48	
49	- **O(1) record path** — clz + shift + saturating counter
50	  increment; no floats, no allocation, no panics, no
51	  data-dependent work. A recorded sample costs zero bytes;
52	  the footprint is fixed at creation.
53	- **Per-instance sizing** — each histogram picks its own
54	  `(grouping_power, max_value_power, count type)`; footprints
55	  range from ~250 B (coarse embedded) to ~250 KB (3-sigfig
56	  benchmarking), with u32 counts the default. See the
57	  [size-tradeoff table](ARCHITECTURE.md#size-tradeoffs).
58	- **Caller-supplied storage** — the core borrows its counts
59	  slice, keeping a future buffer-swap servicing model open
60	  (a background task exchanging a fresh buffer for a full
61	  one) without API changes.
62	- **`no_std` core, `std` convenience** — analysis (quantiles,
63	  merge, iteration) stays `no_std`-capable but off the hot
64	  path; anything needing std is feature-gated.
65	
66	## Initial design
67	
68	The [h2](https://h2histogram.org/) parameterization
69	(`grouping_power`, `max_value_power`)
70	restates HdrHistogram's log-linear bucketing with cleaner
71	integer math: relative error ≤ 2⁻ᵍ, max value 2ⁿ−1, and
72	`buckets = (n−g+1)·2ᵍ`. Correctness is proven against two
73	oracles — iopsystems `histogram` (exact index parity, same
74	scheme) and `hdrhistogram` 7 (quantile parity within
75	equivalent-value tolerance). Full design, API sketch, and
76	tradeoffs: [ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md).
77	
78	## Relation to iopsystems' `histogram`
79	
80	The bucketing scheme is theirs: [h2](https://h2histogram.org/),
81	a base-2 redesign of HdrHistogram, and their
82	[histogram](https://github.com/iopsystems/histogram) crate is
83	one of this crate's two correctness oracles. The two differ in
84	scope, not quality:

# h2hist

A `no_std`, no-alloc implementation of iopsystems'
[h2 histogram](https://h2histogram.org/) — their base-2
redesign of [HdrHistogram](http://hdrhistogram.org/)'s
log-linear bucketing — for continuous monitoring of latency,
jitter, and real-time lateness. Built to be the core recording
structure of [tprobe](../tprobe), but standalone and generally
usable; see
[Relation to iopsystems' histogram](#relation-to-iopsystems-histogram)
for how it differs from their crate.

Status: 0.1.0 cycle in progress (see [TODO.md](TODO.md));
core, analysis, oracle tests, demo, no_std check, and bench
have landed.

## Build and test

- `cargo build` — host build (std feature on by default).
- `cargo build --no-default-features` — the `no_std`
  configuration.
- `cargo test` — unit tests, doctests, and the oracle parity
  suite (dev-deps on `hdrhistogram` and iopsystems
  `histogram` as correctness oracles).

## Demo

`examples/h2demo.rs` records 1M synthetic latency ticks and
prints an iiac-perf-style band table (z/p/n quantile bands
with first/last/range/count/mean, overall and tail-trimmed
mean/stdev):

- `cargo run --example h2demo` — run in place.
- `cargo install --path . --example h2demo` — install the
  demo as a `h2demo` binary.

## no_std check and bench

- `./scripts/check-no-std.sh` — builds the core
  (`--no-default-features`) for every installed bare-metal
  (`*-none-*`) target.
- `cargo bench` — record-path cost vs a raw store and
  `hdrhistogram` (hand-rolled harness; indicative numbers
  and the resulting design decision are in
  [chores-01](notes/chores/chores-01.md)).

## Goals

- **O(1) record path** — clz + shift + saturating counter
  increment; no floats, no allocation, no panics, no
  data-dependent work. A recorded sample costs zero bytes;
  the footprint is fixed at creation.
- **Per-instance sizing** — each histogram picks its own
  `(grouping_power, max_value_power, count type)`; footprints
  range from ~250 B (coarse embedded) to ~250 KB (3-sigfig
  benchmarking), with u32 counts the default. See the
  [size-tradeoff table](ARCHITECTURE.md#size-tradeoffs).
- **Caller-supplied storage** — the core borrows its counts
  slice, keeping a future buffer-swap servicing model open
  (a background task exchanging a fresh buffer for a full
  one) without API changes.
- **`no_std` core, `std` convenience** — analysis (quantiles,
  merge, iteration) stays `no_std`-capable but off the hot
  path; anything needing std is feature-gated.

## Initial design

The [h2](https://h2histogram.org/) parameterization
(`grouping_power`, `max_value_power`)
restates HdrHistogram's log-linear bucketing with cleaner
integer math: relative error ≤ 2⁻ᵍ, max value 2ⁿ−1, and
`buckets = (n−g+1)·2ᵍ`. Correctness is proven against two
oracles — iopsystems `histogram` (exact index parity, same
scheme) and `hdrhistogram` 7 (quantile parity within
equivalent-value tolerance). Full design, API sketch, and
tradeoffs: [ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md).

## Relation to iopsystems' `histogram`

The bucketing scheme is theirs: [h2](https://h2histogram.org/),
a base-2 redesign of HdrHistogram, and their
[histogram](https://github.com/iopsystems/histogram) crate is
one of this crate's two correctness oracles. The two differ in
scope, not quality:

- **iopsystems `histogram`** — std, heap-allocated counts
  (`Box<[Count]>`), atomic / sparse / cumulative-read-only
  variants, serde support; built for services and telemetry
  pipelines. Dependencies: `thiserror` (mandatory),
  `serde` / `schemars` (optional).
- **this crate** — `no_std`, no-alloc core with
  caller-supplied storage, u8–u64 saturating counters,
  clamp-on-over-range record path, zero dependencies; built
  for embedded and real-time recording where heap allocation
  and per-record `Result`s are unaffordable.

Pick theirs on a host with std; pick this one when the
histogram must live in a static buffer.

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

input.new_string:
## no_std check

- `./scripts/check-no-std.sh` — builds the core
  (`--no-default-features`) for every installed bare-metal
  (`*-none-*`) target.

## Bench

`cargo bench --bench record` times the record path over a
pre-generated heavy-tailed stream (8M samples, `g=7 n=30`,
3072 buckets) and reports best-of-3 ns/record per variant
(hand-rolled harness, no criterion). Indicative output
(AMD Ryzen 9 3900X):

```text
raw streaming store       0.961 ns/record
h2hist u32                1.834 ns/record
h2hist u32 + total        2.067 ns/record
h2hist u64                1.838 ns/record
index_for + wrap u32      1.846 ns/record
h2hist u32 + extras       2.559 ns/record
histogram u64             1.827 ns/record
hdrhistogram 2sf          4.943 ns/record
```

The rows and how to read them:

- `raw streaming store` — the floor: each sample written to
  the next slot of a preallocated `Vec<u64>`, no bucketing.
  The gap from here to any histogram row is what that
  histogram's bucketing costs.
- `h2hist u32` — this crate as shipped: index + saturating
  u32 bucket increment, nothing else (totals are summed at
  read time, off the hot path).
- `h2hist u32 + total` — diagnostic, not an API: the same
  plus a caller-side running `saturating_add(1)` per record.
  Documents the measured cost of the retired always-on total
  and prices the keep-your-own-counter pattern for callers
  who want a live total.
- `h2hist u64` — counter-width check: u64 counters bench the
  same as u32, so u32 stays the default on footprint alone.
- `index_for + wrap u32` — diagnostic, not an API:
  `Config::index_for` plus a plain wrapping add on a bare
  slice — the index math alone. Matching `h2hist u32` shows
  the saturating counter update costs nothing measurable.
- `h2hist u32 + extras` — the same plus inline min/max/sum
  tracking: the data for the parked hot-path-extras
  decision.
- `histogram u64` — iopsystems `histogram`, the same h2
  scheme (the peer comparison; u64 heap-allocated counts).
- `hdrhistogram 2sf` — the reference implementation at 2
  significant figures (relative error ≤ 1%, close to g=7's
  ≤ 0.78%), so the comparison runs at like precision.

Caveats: a tight-loop microbench. Code-layout shifts between
recompiles move individual rows by ~±0.2 ns, so compare rows
within one run, not across binaries; treat the numbers as
indicative, not rigorous. History and the resulting design
decisions are in [chores-01](notes/chores/chores-01.md).

message.content: Is the "raw streaming store" u32 or u64 (on x86_64 probably doesn't matter) but on a ARM coretex or other embedded CPU it does?

content.text:
It's u64 — `let mut store = vec![0u64; LEN]` (benches/record.rs:81), and it stores the raw *sample values*, which are u64 throughout the bench. That width choice follows from what the row represents: the "keep every raw sample" alternative (the ArrayRecorder pattern), and a sample there is whatever your tick type is — u64 in this suite. It's not a counter array, so the u32-vs-u64 counter question doesn't apply to that row.

But your instinct is right about where width bites on embedded, with two distinct effects:

- **The store row itself**: on a 32-bit Cortex-M, a u64 store is two word stores (or an `strd`), so the raw-store floor would roughly double relative to a u32 store, and the buffer costs 8 bytes/sample — which is the real killer, since "log every raw sample" is exactly the pattern a small-RAM target can't afford. That's the argument the row exists to make.
- **The histogram rows**: this is where counter width genuinely matters on 32-bit targets — a u64 saturating increment becomes an add/add-with-carry pair plus wider loads/stores, and the counts array doubles (12 KB vs 24 KB at g=7 n=30, before even asking whether that fits). That's precisely why u32 (or u16/u8) is the crate default and the `Counter` trait goes down to u8.

The bigger caveat: this bench only runs on the std host — all the numbers are Ryzen numbers, and none of the x86_64 conclusions (e.g. "saturating add costs nothing", "u64 benches the same as u32") automatically carry to a Cortex-M. On-target measurement would be its own exercise against the `no_std` build. If you want, I can add a sentence to the README's caveats noting that the numbers are host-class only and that counter width matters on 32-bit targets even though it's invisible here.

The plain version: that baseline row copies the full-size 8-byte samples, because it stands for "just save everything raw." On a big PC the size barely matters; on a small 32-bit chip, 8-byte writes cost double and the memory adds up fast — which is exactly why the histogram (with small 4-byte-or-less counters) is the embedded-friendly option, and why numbers from this PC bench shouldn't be assumed to hold on a microcontroller.

# h2hist

A `no_std`, no-alloc implementation of iopsystems'
[h2 histogram](https://h2histogram.org/) — their base-2
redesign of [HdrHistogram](http://hdrhistogram.org/)'s
log-linear bucketing — for continuous monitoring of latency,
jitter, and real-time lateness. Built to be the core recording
structure of [tprobe](../tprobe), but standalone and generally
usable; see
[Relation to iopsystems' histogram](#relation-to-iopsystems-histogram)
for how it differs from their crate.

Status: 0.1.0 cycle in progress (see [TODO.md](TODO.md));
core, analysis, oracle tests, demo, no_std check, and bench
have landed.

## Build and test

- `cargo build` — host build (std feature on by default).
- `cargo build --no-default-features` — the `no_std`
  configuration.
- `cargo test` — unit tests, doctests, and the oracle parity
  suite (dev-deps on `hdrhistogram` and iopsystems
  `histogram` as correctness oracles).

## Demo

`examples/h2demo.rs` records 1M synthetic latency ticks and
prints an iiac-perf-style band table (z/p/n quantile bands
with first/last/range/count/mean, overall and tail-trimmed
mean/stdev):

- `cargo run --example h2demo` — run in place.
- `cargo install --path . --example h2demo` — install the
  demo as a `h2demo` binary.

## no_std check

- `./scripts/check-no-std.sh` — builds the core
  (`--no-default-features`) for every installed bare-metal
  (`*-none-*`) target.

## Bench

`cargo bench --bench record` times the record path over a
pre-generated heavy-tailed stream (8M samples, `g=7 n=30`,
3072 buckets) and reports best-of-3 ns/record per variant
(hand-rolled harness, no criterion). Indicative output
(AMD Ryzen 9 3900X):

```text
raw streaming store       0.961 ns/record
h2hist u32                1.834 ns/record
h2hist u32 + total        2.067 ns/record
h2hist u64                1.838 ns/record
index_for + wrap u32      1.846 ns/record
h2hist u32 + extras       2.559 ns/record
histogram u64             1.827 ns/record
hdrhistogram 2sf          4.943 ns/record
```

The rows and how to read them:

- `raw streaming store` — the floor: each sample written to
  the next slot of a preallocated `Vec<u64>`, no bucketing.
  The gap from here to any histogram row is what that
  histogram's bucketing costs.
- `h2hist u32` — this crate as shipped: index + saturating
  u32 bucket increment, nothing else (totals are summed at
  read time, off the hot path).
- `h2hist u32 + total` — diagnostic, not an API: the same
  plus a caller-side running `saturating_add(1)` per record.
  Documents the measured cost of the retired always-on total
  and prices the keep-your-own-counter pattern for callers
  who want a live total.
- `h2hist u64` — counter-width check: u64 counters bench the
  same as u32, so u32 stays the default on footprint alone.
- `index_for + wrap u32` — diagnostic, not an API:
  `Config::index_for` plus a plain wrapping add on a bare
  slice — the index math alone. Matching `h2hist u32` shows
  the saturating counter update costs nothing measurable.
- `h2hist u32 + extras` — the same plus inline min/max/sum
  tracking: the data for the parked hot-path-extras
  decision.
- `histogram u64` — iopsystems `histogram`, the same h2
  scheme (the peer comparison; u64 heap-allocated counts).
- `hdrhistogram 2sf` — the reference implementation at 2
  significant figures (relative error ≤ 1%, close to g=7's
  ≤ 0.78%), so the comparison runs at like precision.

Caveats: a tight-loop microbench. Code-layout shifts between
recompiles move individual rows by ~±0.2 ns, so compare rows
within one run, not across binaries; treat the numbers as
indicative, not rigorous. History and the resulting design
decisions are in [chores-01](notes/chores/chores-01.md).

## Goals

- **O(1) record path** — clz + shift + saturating counter
  increment; no floats, no allocation, no panics, no
  data-dependent work. A recorded sample costs zero bytes;
  the footprint is fixed at creation.
- **Per-instance sizing** — each histogram picks its own
  `(grouping_power, max_value_power, count type)`; footprints
  range from ~250 B (coarse embedded) to ~250 KB (3-sigfig
  benchmarking), with u32 counts the default. See the
  [size-tradeoff table](ARCHITECTURE.md#size-tradeoffs).
- **Caller-supplied storage** — the core borrows its counts
  slice, keeping a future buffer-swap servicing model open
  (a background task exchanging a fresh buffer for a full
  one) without API changes.
- **`no_std` core, `std` convenience** — analysis (quantiles,
  merge, iteration) stays `no_std`-capable but off the hot
  path; anything needing std is feature-gated.

## Initial design

The [h2](https://h2histogram.org/) parameterization
(`grouping_power`, `max_value_power`)
restates HdrHistogram's log-linear bucketing with cleaner
integer math: relative error ≤ 2⁻ᵍ, max value 2ⁿ−1, and
`buckets = (n−g+1)·2ᵍ`. Correctness is proven against two
oracles — iopsystems `histogram` (exact index parity, same
scheme) and `hdrhistogram` 7 (quantile parity within
equivalent-value tolerance). Full design, API sketch, and
tradeoffs: [ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md).

## Relation to iopsystems' `histogram`

The bucketing scheme is theirs: [h2](https://h2histogram.org/),
a base-2 redesign of HdrHistogram, and their
[histogram](https://github.com/iopsystems/histogram) crate is
one of this crate's two correctness oracles. The two differ in
scope, not quality:

- **iopsystems `histogram`** — std, heap-allocated counts
  (`Box<[Count]>`), atomic / sparse / cumulative-read-only
  variants, serde support; built for services and telemetry
  pipelines. Dependencies: `thiserror` (mandatory),
  `serde` / `schemars` (optional).
- **this crate** — `no_std`, no-alloc core with
  caller-supplied storage, u8–u64 saturating counters,
  clamp-on-over-range record path, zero dependencies; built
  for embedded and real-time recording where heap allocation
  and per-record `Result`s are unaffordable.

Pick theirs on a host with std; pick this one when the
histogram must live in a static buffer.

- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

The `stored` column is in. What changed:

- `bench()` now takes a `stored` argument and the table prints three columns — variant, stored, ns/record — with a header line, so every row states its per-record write width (`u64 sample` for the raw store, `u32 counter`/`u64 counter` for the histograms).
- README's sample output is refreshed to the new format, with a paragraph explaining the column: near-invisible on the x86_64 host, but the number that matters on a 32-bit target (double-word stores, doubled counts array), plus the explicit "these host numbers don't transfer to embedded; measure on target" warning.

One thing to be transparent about: this recompile rolled unlucky code layout for the `h2hist u32` row — it reads ~2.13 in the current binary versus the 1.83 you saw. I verified it's not a regression from the edits: the inner loops are byte-identical, and the `index_for + wrap u32` row doing the same core work still reads ~1.92. It's exactly the ±0.2 ns cross-binary caveat the README documents, and the numbers I pasted into the README are the honest output of the current binary. If that bothers you, the structural fix would be a harness improvement (e.g. interleaving passes or aligning hot loops), which could be a follow-on.

# h2hist

A `no_std`, no-alloc implementation of iopsystems'
[h2 histogram](https://h2histogram.org/) — their base-2
redesign of [HdrHistogram](http://hdrhistogram.org/)'s
log-linear bucketing — for continuous monitoring of latency,
jitter, and real-time lateness. Built to be the core recording
structure of [tprobe](../tprobe), but standalone and generally
usable; see
[Relation to iopsystems' histogram](#relation-to-iopsystems-histogram)
for how it differs from their crate.

Status: 0.1.0 cycle in progress (see [TODO.md](TODO.md));
core, analysis, oracle tests, demo, no_std check, and bench
have landed.

## Build and test

- `cargo build` — host build (std feature on by default).
- `cargo build --no-default-features` — the `no_std`
  configuration.
- `cargo test` — unit tests, doctests, and the oracle parity
  suite (dev-deps on `hdrhistogram` and iopsystems
  `histogram` as correctness oracles).

## Demo

`examples/h2demo.rs` records 1M synthetic latency ticks and
prints an iiac-perf-style band table (z/p/n quantile bands
with first/last/range/count/mean, overall and tail-trimmed
mean/stdev):

- `cargo run --example h2demo` — run in place.
- `cargo install --path . --example h2demo` — install the
  demo as a `h2demo` binary.

## no_std check

- `./scripts/check-no-std.sh` — builds the core
  (`--no-default-features`) for every installed bare-metal
  (`*-none-*`) target.

## Bench

`cargo bench --bench record` times the record path over a
pre-generated heavy-tailed stream (8M samples, `g=7 n=30`,
3072 buckets) and reports best-of-3 ns/record per variant
(hand-rolled harness, no criterion). Indicative output
(AMD Ryzen 9 3900X):

```text
variant                stored       ns/record
raw streaming store    u64 sample       1.002
h2hist u32             u32 counter      2.126
h2hist u32 + total     u32 counter      2.128
h2hist u64             u64 counter      1.966
index_for + wrap u32   u32 counter      1.915
h2hist u32 + extras    u32 counter      2.586
histogram u64          u64 counter      1.856
hdrhistogram 2sf       u64 counter      5.004
```

The `stored` column names what the variant writes per
record. On the x86_64 host the width is near-invisible, but
it is the number that matters on a 32-bit target (a u64
store/increment is two words on a Cortex-M, and the counts
array doubles) — these host numbers do not transfer to
embedded CPUs; measure on target.

The rows and how to read them:

- `raw streaming store` — the floor: each sample written to
  the next slot of a preallocated `Vec<u64>`, no bucketing.
  The gap from here to any histogram row is what that
  histogram's bucketing costs.
- `h2hist u32` — this crate as shipped: index + saturating
  u32 bucket increment, nothing else (totals are summed at
  read time, off the hot path).
- `h2hist u32 + total` — diagnostic, not an API: the same
  plus a caller-side running `saturating_add(1)` per record.
  Documents the measured cost of the retired always-on total
  and prices the keep-your-own-counter pattern for callers
  who want a live total.
- `h2hist u64` — counter-width check: u64 counters bench the
  same as u32, so u32 stays the default on footprint alone.
- `index_for + wrap u32` — diagnostic, not an API:
  `Config::index_for` plus a plain wrapping add on a bare
  slice — the index math alone. Matching `h2hist u32` shows
  the saturating counter update costs nothing measurable.
- `h2hist u32 + extras` — the same plus inline min/max/sum
  tracking: the data for the parked hot-path-extras
  decision.
- `histogram u64` — iopsystems `histogram`, the same h2
  scheme (the peer comparison; u64 heap-allocated counts).
- `hdrhistogram 2sf` — the reference implementation at 2
  significant figures (relative error ≤ 1%, close to g=7's
  ≤ 0.78%), so the comparison runs at like precision.

Caveats: a tight-loop microbench. Code-layout shifts between
recompiles move individual rows by ~±0.2 ns, so compare rows
within one run, not across binaries; treat the numbers as
indicative, not rigorous. History and the resulting design
decisions are in [chores-01](notes/chores/chores-01.md).

## Goals

- **O(1) record path** — clz + shift + saturating counter
  increment; no floats, no allocation, no panics, no
  data-dependent work. A recorded sample costs zero bytes;
  the footprint is fixed at creation.
- **Per-instance sizing** — each histogram picks its own
  `(grouping_power, max_value_power, count type)`; footprints
  range from ~250 B (coarse embedded) to ~250 KB (3-sigfig
  benchmarking), with u32 counts the default. See the
  [size-tradeoff table](ARCHITECTURE.md#size-tradeoffs).
- **Caller-supplied storage** — the core borrows its counts
  slice, keeping a future buffer-swap servicing model open
  (a background task exchanging a fresh buffer for a full
  one) without API changes.
- **`no_std` core, `std` convenience** — analysis (quantiles,
  merge, iteration) stays `no_std`-capable but off the hot
  path; anything needing std is feature-gated.

## Initial design

The [h2](https://h2histogram.org/) parameterization
(`grouping_power`, `max_value_power`)
restates HdrHistogram's log-linear bucketing with cleaner
integer math: relative error ≤ 2⁻ᵍ, max value 2ⁿ−1, and
`buckets = (n−g+1)·2ᵍ`. Correctness is proven against two
oracles — iopsystems `histogram` (exact index parity, same
scheme) and `hdrhistogram` 7 (quantile parity within
equivalent-value tolerance). Full design, API sketch, and
tradeoffs: [ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md).

## Relation to iopsystems' `histogram`

The bucketing scheme is theirs: [h2](https://h2histogram.org/),
a base-2 redesign of HdrHistogram, and their
[histogram](https://github.com/iopsystems/histogram) crate is
one of this crate's two correctness oracles. The two differ in
scope, not quality:

- **iopsystems `histogram`** — std, heap-allocated counts
  (`Box<[Count]>`), atomic / sparse / cumulative-read-only
  variants, serde support; built for services and telemetry
  pipelines. Dependencies: `thiserror` (mandatory),
  `serde` / `schemars` (optional).
- **this crate** — `no_std`, no-alloc core with
  caller-supplied storage, u8–u64 saturating counters,
  clamp-on-over-range record path, zero dependencies; built
  for embedded and real-time recording where heap allocation
  and per-record `Result`s are unaffordable.

Pick theirs on a host with std; pick this one when the
histogram must live in a static buffer.

- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted

## Goals

- **O(1) record path** — clz + shift + saturating counter
  increment; no floats, no allocation, no panics, no
  data-dependent work. A recorded sample costs zero bytes;
  the footprint is fixed at creation.
- **Per-instance sizing** — each histogram picks its own
  `(grouping_power, max_value_power, count type)`; footprints
  range from ~250 B (coarse embedded) to ~250 KB (3-sigfig
  benchmarking), with u32 counts the default. See the
  [size-tradeoff table](ARCHITECTURE.md#size-tradeoffs).
- **Caller-supplied storage** — the core borrows its counts
  slice, keeping a future buffer-swap servicing model open
  (a background task exchanging a fresh buffer for a full
  one) without API changes.
- **`no_std` core, `std` convenience** — analysis (quantiles,
  merge, iteration) stays `no_std`-capable but off the hot
  path; anything needing std is feature-gated.

## Initial design

The [h2](https://h2histogram.org/) parameterization
(`grouping_power`, `max_value_power`)
restates HdrHistogram's log-linear bucketing with cleaner
integer math: relative error ≤ 2⁻ᵍ, max value 2ⁿ−1, and
`buckets = (n−g+1)·2ᵍ`. Correctness is proven against two
oracles — iopsystems `histogram` (exact index parity, same
scheme) and `hdrhistogram` 7 (quantile parity within
equivalent-value tolerance). Full design, API sketch, and
tradeoffs: [ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md).

## Relation to iopsystems' `histogram`

The bucketing scheme is theirs: [h2](https://h2histogram.org/),
a base-2 redesign of HdrHistogram, and their
[histogram](https://github.com/iopsystems/histogram) crate is
one of this crate's two correctness oracles. The two differ in
scope, not quality:

- **iopsystems `histogram`** — std, heap-allocated counts
  (`Box<[Count]>`), atomic / sparse / cumulative-read-only
  variants, serde support; built for services and telemetry
  pipelines. Dependencies: `thiserror` (mandatory),
  `serde` / `schemars` (optional).
- **this crate** — `no_std`, no-alloc core with
  caller-supplied storage, u8–u64 saturating counters,
  clamp-on-over-range record path, zero dependencies; built
  for embedded and real-time recording where heap allocation
  and per-record `Result`s are unaffordable.

Pick theirs on a host with std; pick this one when the
histogram must live in a static buffer.

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

aiTitle: Compare performance between h2hist, histogram and HdrHistogram

content.text:
Your observation cracked it open — chasing that consistent gap led to the real bottleneck, and the result is dramatic. Final table (stable across runs, all checks green):

| variant | ns/record |
|---|---|
| raw streaming store | 0.93–1.02 |
| **h2hist u32** | **0.88–0.90** |
| h2hist u32 + total | 0.98 |
| h2hist u64 | 0.86–0.88 |
| index_for + wrap u32 | 0.81–0.94 |
| h2hist u32 + extras | 1.31 |
| histogram u64 | 1.61–1.65 |
| hdrhistogram 2sf | 4.97 |

What the investigation found, in order:

- The duplicate-loop experiment and a forced-alignment build (`-align-all-blocks`) showed the ~1.85-vs-2.13 swings were code placement, but a real gap remained under aligned conditions.
- Disassembly then revealed the actual problem: **`Config::index_for` was never being inlined** — it's non-generic with no `#[inline]`, so the bench (an external crate, like any h2hist user without LTO) made an indirect function call *per record*, with field reloads every iteration. The saturating increment, meanwhile, compiled to a single `inc`+`cmove` — effectively free, which is why all those counter experiments showed nothing.
- Fix: `#[inline]` on `Config`'s small const fns (src/config.rs). Verified in the disassembly: the loop is now call-free with the clz inlined.
- I also tried a branch-shaped `sat_add`; LLVM emits identical code either way, so I reverted counter.rs to the original simpler form.

Net effect: **2.57 → 0.89 ns/record today (2.9×)** — h2hist now beats the raw streaming store (its 12 KB counts stay in L1 while the raw store streams 64 MB through DRAM), and is ~1.8× faster than the histogram crate. The README sample table is refreshed and the raw-store row's description corrected (it's not a floor). The `#[inline]` finding is also the most practically important one for tprobe: without it, any non-LTO downstream crate was paying that call on every record.

Cleanup is done (temp asm-diff functions and the `copy b` row removed), and everything passes: fmt, clippy, full tests, oracle parity, no_std targets. The pending pile for a cycle is now: `index_for` reorder + `#[inline]`s, total removal, bench variants/columns, README/ARCHITECTURE updates.

The plain version: your repeated runs showed our histogram was reliably a bit slower than it should be. The cause wasn't the math — it was that the compiler was making a full function call for every sample instead of pasting the small indexing code directly into the loop. One-word annotations (`#[inline]`) fixed it. Recording is now about three times faster than when we started today, faster than even just writing every sample to memory, and clearly ahead of both comparison libraries.
